retrieved_docs.title: Interferon -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Interferons (IFNs) are proteins that belong to the group of signaling molecules known as cytokines involved in the upregulation of the immune response. Interferons are particularly important in fighting viral infections but also play a vital role in tumor suppression, upregulation of MHC Class 1 and 2, signal transduction, and activation of immune cells, including natural killer cells and macrophages. Interferons can broadly be classified into three main subtypes, interferon-alpha, beta, and gamma, with interferon-alpha and beta belonging to the Type 1 interferon subclass and interferon-gamma belonging to the Type 2 subclass. [11] More recently, there has been the discovery of a third subclass of interferons, namely Type 3, which includes interferon lambda. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon-Induced Retinopathy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Interferons constitute a family of proteins released by various cells in response to viral infections. They are crucial in promoting innate and acquired immune responses to restrict viral propagation. In addition, interferons can combat bacterial and parasitic infections, inhibit cell division, and promote or impede the differentiation of cells. The 3 main classes of interferons are interferon-α, interferon-β, and interferon-γ . These interferons activate natural killer cells and macrophages and also increase host defenses. They achieve this by upregulating antigen presentation through an increase in the expression of major histocompatibility complex antigens. Following secretion, interferons bind to receptors on neighboring cells, prompting the production of antiviral proteins and additional interferons.

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, interferons have significant immunomodulatory effects, with interferon-gamma being the predominant immunomodulatory interferon. Type 1 interferons act as antiviral cytokines. Alpha interferons activate NK cells, which in turn destroy viral and other targets; increase the proliferation of B cells, which secrete antibodies against pathogens; and enhance CD8 T cell response by upregulating the expression of MHC class 1 on surface cells. Type 1 interferons also upregulate IL-12Rb and increase the expression of MHC class II molecules. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Function -- Immune Response to Viruses
retrieved_docs.content: Interferons, natural killer cells, and phagocytes prevent the spread of viruses in the early stages of infection. Specific antibodies and complement proteins participate in viral neutralization and can limit the spread and reinfection. Adaptive immunity is crucial for protecting the body against viruses, including CD8+ T cells, which kill infected cells, and CD4+ T cells, which serve as the dominant effector cell population in response to many virus infections. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Interferons exhibit immune-modulatory effects by initiating signaling cascades that lead to the expression of gene products such as MHC class 1, B2 microglobulin, and others. Alpha and beta interferons bind to the IFNA receptor, which has two parts, IFNAR1 and IFNAR2. IFNAR1 has a low affinity for interferon on its own, but binding is enhanced when accompanied by IFNAR2. Phosphatases SHP-1 and 2 associate with IFNAR1 and exhibit negative feedback in the activation of JAK signaling. IFNAR2 has three variants, short, soluble, and long-form. The long-form leads to activation of the JAK-STAT pathway and antiviral response. When stimulated by interferon, protein complexes form and translocate to the nucleus and activate STATs. This, in turn, leads to the dimerization of IFNAR1 and IFNAR2, which triggers a phosphorylation cascade. First, the JAK kinase, Tyk 2, which is associated with IFNAR1, is immediately phosphorylated by JAK1, another JAK kinase bound to IFNAR2. Activation of Tyk2 then phosphorylates JAK1, leading to phosphorylation of IFNAR1 and 2. Next, STAT2 binds to IFNAR1 at specific phosphorylated residues. Afterward, STAT2 is phosphorylated by JAK kinases, creating a port for STAT1, which is also phosphorylated. After becoming phosphorylated, the STATS dissociate and bind to the interferon regulatory factor 9, which forms the major interferon transcription factor, ISGF-3. IGSF-3 then translocates to the nucleus and binds to ISRE, initiating transcription of interferon-inducible genes. For interferon-gamma, there are different DNA regulatory sequences called gamma-activated sequence elements, which are present in promoters of interferon-gamma stimulated genes. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Interferons exhibit antiviral activity at many stages of the viral replication cycle, including entry, transcription, RNA stability, translation, maturation, and release. This action is under the mediation of the expression of antiviral genes. Interferons stimulate the expression of PKR through an ISRE and GAS in the promoter of the PKR gene. The kinase activity of the PKR gene, in turn, phosphorylates the translation initiation factor eIF2-a at Ser51. eIF2-a-GTP is necessary for the initiation of viral translation. PKR also plays roles in cell proliferation, tumor suppression, and signal transduction through the regulation of serine phosphorylation of STAT1 and the phosphorylation of IkB, which leads to the activation of NF-kB-dependent genes. Additionally, the 2-5 A oligosynthetase/RNAse L system is strongly induced by interferons. RNAse Ls are activated by double-stranded RNAs and degrade all single-stranded RNA, thereby inhibiting viral replication. The Mx proteins are a family of GTPases induced by interferons and assemble into oligomeric and interfere with transcription in negative-sense virus replication. An additional protein involved in inhibiting viral replication that is induced by interferons is the guanylate binding protein. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon Test -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Interferon-γ is the primary cytokine released in response to infection by M tuberculosis bacteria. Macrophages are the first immune cells to respond to infection. Following phagocytosis, macrophages secrete cytokines to attract T helper cells, the primary mediators of interferon-γ. Interferon-γ further amplifies the activation of macrophages. This activation-reactivation process between macrophages and T helper cells occurs repeatedly as a positive feedback loop until the infection is eradicated or dormant. IGRAs quantify the immune reaction of T helper cells to detect latent TB. Fresh blood samples are mixed with antigens and controls to determine if infection is present. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Macrophages produce lysosomal enzymes and reactive oxygen species to eliminate the ingested pathogens. These cells produce cytokines that attract other leukocytes to the site of infection. The innate response to viruses includes synthesizing and releasing interferons and activating natural killer cells that recognize and destroy the virus-infected cells. The innate immunity against bacteria consists of activating neutrophils that ingest pathogens and moving monocytes to the inflamed tissue, where they become macrophages. These macrophages can engulf and process the antigen and present it to activated specialized cells. Eosinophils protect against parasitic infections by releasing the content of their granules. Antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity: A cytotoxic reaction where Fc-receptor-expressing killer cells recognize target cells through specific antibodies. Affinity maturation: The increase in average antibody affinity is mostly observed during a secondary immune response.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The immune response is a complex and highly coordinated process that the body uses to defend against pathogens such as bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites. This system comprises a network of cells, tissues, and organs that work in concert to identify and neutralize these harmful invaders. Understanding the immune response mechanism involves exploring innate (nonspecific) and adaptive (specific) immunity and the various components and pathways involved in recognizing and responding to pathogens.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Function -- Immune Response to Fungi
retrieved_docs.content: The innate immunity to fungi includes defensins and phagocytes. CD4+ T-helper cells are responsible for the adaptive immune response against fungi. Dendritic cells secrete interleukin-12 (IL-12) after ingesting fungi, and IL-12 activates the synthesis of gamma interferon, which activates cell-mediated immunity. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Mallory Bodies -- Mechanisms
retrieved_docs.content: The 3rd mechanism involves chronic activation of toll-like receptors (TLRs), which stimulate proinflammatory and cell growth pathways. Inflammatory cytokines, such as tumor necrosis factor α (TNF-α) and interferon γ (IFN-γ), activate TLR signaling, promoting the upregulation of the immunoproteasome and downregulation of the 26S proteasome. This imbalance further hinders protein degradation and contributes to the accumulation of MDB. Agents such as DDC not only disrupt proteasome function but also enhance TLR signaling by increasing tumor necrosis factor α and interferon γ expression, creating a feedback loop that amplifies cellular stress and MDB formation. [21] [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Function -- Innate Immunity
retrieved_docs.content: Cellular components Various cell types play critical roles in the innate immune response. Phagocytes, including macrophages, neutrophils, and dendritic cells, engulf and digest pathogens through phagocytosis.Natural killer cells can recognize and kill infected or cancerous cells by inducing apoptosis, also known as programmed cell death. Mast cells and basophils release histamines and other chemicals during inflammatory responses, increasing blood flow and recruiting other immune cells to the site of infection. Soluble Mediators Cytokines: These signaling molecules, such as interleukins and interferons, coordinate the immune response by promoting cell communication and activating immune cells. Complement systems are a group of proteins that, when activated, can directly kill pathogens or facilitate phagocyte uptake through opsonization. Acute phase proteins such as c-reactive protein, which increase in response to infection and inflammation, enhance the immune response. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: TH1: The response stimulated by the release of IL-12 from dendritic cells and macrophages; secretes IL-2, IFN-gamma, and TNF-beta; inhibited by IL-4 and IL-10; provides defense against intracellular infections and fungi

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon Test -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Bacilli tubers invade the upper respiratory tract and travel in small (5 to 10 microns) droplets to the alveolar spaces. When the host's immune system does not eradicate the bacteria, the bacilli propagate within macrophages and ultimately destroy the cells. Infected macrophages generate inflammatory cytokines and recruit additional phagocytes, forming a nodular, caseating granuloma known as a tubercle. The granuloma prevents the infection from spreading to other areas of the lungs.

retrieved_docs.title: Herpes Simplex Keratitis -- Pathophysiology -- Mechanism of Viral Evasion
retrieved_docs.content: HSV-1 employs many strategies to elude host immune detection and manipulate cellular functions, facilitating its replication and maintenance within the host. A critical examination of the cellular pathways targeted by the virus reveals potential avenues for therapeutic intervention aimed at reinforcing these host defenses. The virus circumvents the host's early warning systems, namely the TLR, RIG-I-like receptor, and cytosolic DNA sensing pathways, which are primary alerts to viral presence. HSV-1 achieves this evasion by impairing the functionality of these sensing mechanisms or their associated signaling cascades, effectively remaining under the radar of host surveillance. A notable tactic involves the viral protein ICP0, which orchestrates the ubiquitination of crucial proteins within the host's defense network, such as the interferon-gamma inducible protein 16. [55] This modification essentially cloaks the virus from the innate immune system's detection. Furthermore, HSV-1 utilizes its viral kinases to modify the phosphorylation patterns of critical transcription factors, obstructing their ability to migrate to the nucleus and activate antiviral gene expression. This manipulation is exemplified in interferon regulatory factor 3 and nuclear factor-kB pathways alteration by the viral kinase US3. Beyond these mechanisms, HSV-1 disrupts a range of essential cellular defense processes, including the presentation of antigens via MHC class I, the DNA damage response, autophagy, the endoplasmic reticulum stress response, and the process of necroptosis. Each viral evasion tactic underscores the sophisticated interplay between HSV-1 and host cellular mechanisms, highlighting critical vulnerabilities within the host's immune defense. By advancing our understanding of these interactions, a promising potential exists to develop therapeutic strategies that enhance the host's cellular responses to combat HSV-1 infection and its persistence. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Research has shown interferon beta to upregulate and downregulate many interferon-dependent gene transcripts (SOCS 1, C-C motif, CCL1, CCL7, CCL2, JAK2, ISG20, and CCL20 were upregulated, while CXCL6 and IL-8 were downregulated). Interferon beta has also been demonstrated to increase levels of IL-10, IL-23A, and IL-5 while decreasing lymphocyte counts, including T cells, CT cells, Th cells, B cells, and NK cells. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 504
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirové látky (antiviral agents) represent a pharmacological class that includes specific drugs such as interferon alfa‑2a, a cytokine‑based medication used to treat a variety of viral infections. Certain antiviral agents are chemically related to deoxyglukosa (deoxyglucose), indicating that deoxyglucose analogues can serve as the basis for antiviral compounds or influence their mechanism of action. Moreover, the antiviral class can be traced to parent compounds like pentamidin (pentamidine), suggesting that some antiviral drugs are derived from, or share structural features with, this older antimicrobial agent. Together, these relationships illustrate how antiviral agents are linked to both biologically active molecules (interferon alfa‑2a) and foundational chemical scaffolds (deoxyglukosa and pentamidin), providing insight into their development, mechanisms, and therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 461
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirové látky (antiviral agents) constitute a therapeutic class of compounds that are linked to prostaglandin A1 and are derived from or share a structural relationship with pentamidin, indicating that pentamidin serves as a parent molecule for this group. Within this class, interferon alfa‑2a is recognized as a specific member, exemplifying the type of biologically active agent that functions as an antiviral. The interconnections among prostaglandin A1, interferon alfa‑2a, and pentamidin illustrate the diverse chemical and pharmacological origins of antivirové látky, highlighting their relevance for developing and selecting antiviral therapies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 119
retrieved_docs.content: A1, suggesting that prostaglandin A1 is considered a type of antiviral agent in some pharmacologic frameworks, while interferon alfa‑2a is an inverse_isa of antivirové látky, placing interferon alfa‑2a as a specific antiviral within this group. Additionally, pentamidin is identified as a parent of antivirové látky, indicating that pentamidin represents an even broader chemical or therapeutic class that includes antiviral agents. These intersecting hierarchies illustrate the complexity of drug classification, where a single agent may belong to multiple categories based on chemical structure, mechanism of action, or therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1949
retrieved_docs.content: Tolpa and acetylmuramyl‑alanyl‑isoglutamin are both classified as immunostimulants, agents that enhance or modulate the activity of the immune system. By acting as immunostimulants, these substances can promote immune cell activation, increase cytokine production, and improve the body’s ability to respond to infections or malignancies, making them relevant in therapeutic strategies aimed at strengthening host defenses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 511
retrieved_docs.content: ANTI‑INFECTIVE AGENTS FOR BACTERIA, VIRUSES AND FUNGI encompass a broad therapeutic category that includes antivirové látky (antiviral agents). Antivirové látky are classified as a type of prostaglandin A1‑related compound and are considered to descend from the parent compound pentamidin. Within this antiviral class, interferon alfa‑2a is a specific agent that exemplifies the therapeutic use of antivirové látky. Understanding that antivirové látky are a subclass of the larger anti‑infective group, linked chemically to prostaglandin A1 and hierarchically derived from pentamidin, helps clinicians organize treatment strategies for infections caused by bacteria, viruses, and fungi.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 579
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirové látky are classified as a type of prostaglandin A1 and encompass agents such as interferon alfa‑2a, indicating that interferon alfa‑2a is an antiviral medication. These antiviral compounds are derived from the parent molecule pentamidin, linking their chemical origin to this precursor. Antivirové látky are directly associated with عدوى فيروسية (viral infection), reflecting their therapeutic purpose in combating viral diseases. In line with this role, the antiviral drug remdesivir may be used to treat عدوى فيروسية, further illustrating the connection between antiviral agents and the management of viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1314
retrieved_docs.content: toxins, are employed to safely stimulate protective immune responses, and xenon, an inert gas with anesthetic properties, influences neuronal activity and cerebral physiology. Collectively, these diverse entities serve as important qualifiers for describing and investigating physiological mechanisms, disease processes, and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylmuramyl-alanyl-isoglutamin is classified as an immunostimulant, meaning it belongs to the group of agents that activate or enhance the body’s immune response. As an immunostimulant, acetylmuramyl-alanyl-isoglutamin can be used to boost immune function in various clinical contexts, supporting the body’s ability to recognize and combat pathogens or abnormal cells.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 281
retrieved_docs.content: Rimantadine is classified as a cyclic amine and a direct‑acting antiviral, placing it within a group of compounds that target viral replication mechanisms. A notable physiologic effect of rimantadine is the alteration of cellular synthetic activity, which can influence both viral production and host cell metabolic processes. Recognizing rimantadine’s membership in the cyclic amine, direct‑acting antiviral class and its capacity to modify cellular synthesis helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, anticipate potential cellular effects, and optimize its use in antiviral therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2002
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirové látky (antiviral agents) represent a heterogeneous group of pharmacologically active substances that include prostaglandin A1, deoxyglukóza (deoxyglucose) and interferon alfa‑2a. Both prostaglandin A1 and deoxyglukóza are classified as specific antiviral agents, while interferon alfa‑2a is a well‑known cytokine that functions as a distinct antiviral drug. In addition, antiviral agents are linked to pentamidin (pentamidine) as a parent compound, indicating that pentamidine serves as a broader chemical or pharmacological precursor within the antiviral drug hierarchy. This interconnected classification underscores the diverse chemical families that can exhibit antiviral activity and highlights the importance of understanding these relationships for drug development and patient‑centered therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: and tecovirimat are specifically indicated for smallpox. Ophthalmic antivirals form a subcategory and include famciclovir, ganciclovir, idoxuridine, trifluridine and vidarabine, all employed for viral eye infections. Ganciclovir, idoxuridine and cidofovir belong to the subclass “nucleosides and nucleotides excluding reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors,” which inhibits nucleic‑acid synthesis; deoxyuridine is the chemical precursor of idoxuridine, and this subclass is linked to the broader chemical family “vidarabin,” to which fludarabine is mapped. Interferon α‑2a is identified as a cytokine‑based antiviral within the same overarching class, while prostaglandin A1 is unusually recorded as a parent class for antiviral agents. Rimantadine is classified both as a cyclic amine and a direct‑acting antiviral, exerting a physiological effect described as alteration of cellular synthetic activity. The antiviral class is also placed under the parent compound pentamidine and is directly associated

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 45
retrieved_docs.content: Antiviral agents used in ophthalmology—including famciclovir, ganciclovir, idoxuridin and, by extension, trifluridin—are classified within the Czech‑language group “antivirové látky,” which is linked to prostaglandin A1, lists interferon α‑2a as a specific antiviral subtype, and is considered to derive from the parent compound pentamidine. This group is directly associated with viral infections (عدوى فيروسية). Most of these ophthalmic antivirals belong to the class “Nucleosides and nucleotides excl. reverse transcriptase inhibitors,” a family of nucleoside or nucleotide analogues that inhibit viral nucleic‑acid synthesis; ganciclovir exemplifies the mechanism “Nucleic Acid Synthesis Inhibitors,” and idoxuridin—derived from deoxyuridine—also prevents viral infection. Additional members of this class are cidofovir, brincidofovir, brivudine (bromodeoxyuridine) and remdesivir, the latter being noted for its potential to treat viral infections. Beyond ocular use, ribavirin may prevent viral infection, while brincidofovir and tecovirimat are indicated for the treatment of smallpox (الجدري), a disease classified as a type of viral infection (نوع من العدوى فيروسية). Collectively, these agents illustrate a hierarchical network in which famciclovir, ganciclovir, idoxuridin, trifluridin, ribavirin, cidofovir, brincidofovir, brivudine, remdesivir and tecovirimat are nucleoside‑based or related antivirals that share the common mechanism of inhibiting viral nucleic‑acid synthesis and are employed either prophylactically (e.g., ribavirin) or therapeutically (e.g., ganciclovir, brincidofovir, tecovirimat) to prevent or treat viral diseases, including ocular viral infections and systemic infections such as smallpox.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1906
retrieved_docs.content: Betamethason is identified as a potent corticosteroid that belongs to the broader group of corticosteroids and other potent combinations, indicating its classification within this class of anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive agents. In addition, betamethason may be employed in the therapeutic management of الصَّدَفِيَّة, a term referring to epidemic or plague‑like conditions, suggesting that this corticosteroid can be used to address severe inflammatory or immune‑mediated aspects of such widespread infectious diseases. This dual characterization underscores betamethason’s role both as a member of a potent corticosteroid class and as a potential treatment option for epidemic‑related medical challenges.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1762
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort is categorized under the therapeutic class Immunological Agents [TC], indicating that it functions as an immunomodulatory medication used to suppress immune activity in various clinical contexts.
